pattern: Each two-letter word is really an order constraint between two letters; a shortest supersequence needs only one letter repeated twice (its earliest and latest occurrence) to satisfy every constraint it touches, so the whole problem reduces to finding the smallest set of letters to duplicate so the remaining letters form an acyclic order — a bitmask search over at most 16 letters.
hints: A word with two different letters says 'this letter must appear somewhere before that one'; a word with two equal letters says 'this letter must appear at least twice'. You never need more than 2 occurrences of any letter: only its first and last occurrence are ever checked by any constraint.

Pick a set S of letters that will be duplicated (appear twice); every other used letter appears once. This works if and only if the directed graph of 'before' constraints, restricted to the letters NOT in S, has no cycle — duplicated letters can always be placed at the very start and very end, so they never conflict with anything.

Since at most 16 distinct letters are ever involved, brute-force every subset of the non-forced letters (letters that must be duplicated because of a same-letter word are always in S) to find the maximum number of letters you can leave un-duplicated while keeping the remaining directed graph acyclic; every subset achieving that maximum gives one distinct frequency vector.

edge_inputs.description: statement example: a two-letter cycle needs one repeat, two ways
statement example: a self-loop forces a repeat, feeding a downstream edge
statement example: three independent self-loops, no ordering edges at all
single word, no constraints beyond using both letters once
a pure DAG chain needs zero repeats: one unique arrangement
a 3-cycle needs exactly one repeated letter, three distinct choices
a fully bidirectional pair on top of an untouched third letter
a fully-connected bidirectional triangle needs two repeats, not one

stress.description: random small directed graphs over a handful of letters, mixing self-loops and both-direction edges

complexity.time: O(2^u * u) where u <= 16 is the number of distinct letters
